provenance.source_corpus: Plant reintroduction in restored peatlands: 80% successfully transferred – Does the remaining 20% matter?

establishment of C. trisperma and I. mucronata

edges.subject: causal_mosaic:node:3b9a1708b232
edges.original_sentence: On average, 82% of the species targeted by the technique e i.e. the species present in the donor site and transferred into the site to be restored e establish successfully.

source_spans.text: On average, 82% of the species targeted by the technique e i.e. the species present in the donor site and transferred into the site to be restored e establish successfully.

source_document.title: Plant reintroduction in restored peatlands: 80% successfully transferred – Does the remaining 20% matter?

edges.subject: causal_mosaic:node:ddbacfe0e7ac
edges.original_sentence: The peat Mg concentration was the only other variable explaining significantly (F ¼ 10.08, P ¼ 0.005) the presence of Recalcitrant Species in restored sites according to the RDA model (not shown, explaining 34% of the variability). Indeed, Mg concentration was at least three times higher in restored sites where C. trisperma, I. mucronata, M. trifolium and R. chamaemorus were present (Table 3).

source_spans.text: The peat Mg concentration was the only other variable explaining significantly (F ¼ 10.08, P ¼ 0.005) the presence of Recalcitrant Species in restored sites according to the RDA model (not shown, explaining 34% of the variability). Indeed, Mg concentration was at least three times higher in restored sites where C. trisperma, I. mucronata, M. trifolium and R. chamaemorus were present (Table 3).

source_document.title: Plant reintroduction in restored peatlands: 80% successfully transferred – Does the remaining 20% matter?

edges.subject: causal_mosaic:node:af312150bb57
edges.original_sentence: M. trifolium and R. chamaemorus reproduce mainly by rhizomes so we believe that the fragmentation of the rhizome during the restoration process is problematic. Donor plant material is fragmented by a rotovator and a manure spreader during the steps of plant collection and introduction, resulting in fragments usually less than 2 cm long deposited on top of the peat. Such short sections of rhizomes are probably not sufficient for the regeneration of new individuals and establishment of the species in the restored sites.

source_spans.text: M. trifolium and R. chamaemorus reproduce mainly by rhizomes so we believe that the fragmentation of the rhizome during the restoration process is problematic. Donor plant material is fragmented by a rotovator and a manure spreader during the steps of plant collection and introduction, resulting in fragments usually less than 2 cm long deposited on top of the peat. Such short sections of rhizomes are probably not sufficient for the regeneration of new individuals and establishment of the species in the restored sites.

source_document.title: Plant reintroduction in restored peatlands: 80% successfully transferred – Does the remaining 20% matter?

edges.subject: causal_mosaic:node:366df7a34852
edges.original_sentence: The lack of such habitats in the relatively young restored sites probably explains why those species were classified as Recalcitrant Species.

edges.original_sentence: the species exhibited a clear preference for older restored sites (10 years and more), which coincides with the known 8 e10 years ’ delay between germination and first flowering of this species ( Curtis, 1943 ).

edges.subject: causal_mosaic:node:20069f83ad22
edges.original_sentence: From our field experience, we learned that fragmentation of the moss carpet by the machinery is beneficial for the establishment of Sphagnum mosses because it ensures that plant material is spread in a uniform thin layer (~1 cm).

source_spans.text: From our field experience, we learned that fragmentation of the moss carpet by the machinery is beneficial for the establishment of Sphagnum mosses because it ensures that plant material is spread in a uniform thin layer (~1 cm).

source_document.title: Plant reintroduction in restored peatlands: 80% successfully transferred – Does the remaining 20% matter?
